methodology: Cross-referenced USDA ingredient data, FatSecret, CalorieKing, and restaurant-published nutrition. Most sushi restaurants do not publish nutrition facts — estimates are built from standardized ingredient weights (sushi rice ~200cal per roll, nori ~5cal per sheet, fish ~40-50cal per oz). Restaurant rolls tend to run 10-20% higher than home-made due to extra rice, sauce, and oil.

key_insights.rice_per_roll: One standard sushi roll contains ~200 cal of seasoned rice (3/4 cup cooked, ~7oz). This is the #1 calorie driver in most rolls.
key_insights.sashimi_advantage: Sashimi is ~35-50 cal per slice (pure protein, zero carbs). 5 slices of tuna sashimi = 155 cal, 33g protein. Same fish in a roll = 300+ cal.
key_insights.spicy_mayo_trap: Spicy mayo adds ~100 cal per tablespoon (almost pure fat). Most specialty rolls get 1-2 tbsp drizzled on top.
key_insights.poke_bowl_illusion: A 'healthy' poke bowl with rice base, avocado, edamame, and sauce easily hits 800-1200 cal. The greens-base version saves 200-300 cal.
key_insights.tempura_tax: Deep frying adds ~100-150 cal per roll from oil absorption in batter. A tempura roll vs non-tempura = the difference of a whole extra roll.

items.notes: Imitation crab, avocado, cucumber, rice, nori. The 'starter' roll. Relatively low cal for a roll but almost no real protein — the crab is processed surimi. FatSecret generic: 220 cal/8pc; RA Sushi: 310; Kona Grill: 350. We use mid-range estimate.

Raw tuna, spicy mayo, rice, nori. Better protein than California roll but the spicy mayo adds hidden fat. Southern Tsunami 8pc: 430 cal (heavy on mayo). Marketside: 200 cal (lean). Mid-range with typical restaurant mayo.
Raw salmon, avocado, rice, nori. Solid protein + healthy fats from both salmon and avocado. One of the better macro rolls if you skip extra sauce. Estimated from USDA salmon + standard sushi rice.
Simple thin roll — raw tuna and rice only. One of the leanest rolls. No avocado, no mayo, no tempura. Great protein-to-cal ratio. USDA-based: tuna is ~31 cal/oz with 7g protein.

The lowest calorie roll — but also the lowest protein. Basically seasoned rice wrapped around cucumber. Fine as a side, but don't count on it for satiety. CalorieKing: 188 cal/6pc; FatSecret generic lower.
Smoked salmon, cream cheese, cucumber, rice. The cream cheese makes this one of the fattier 'basic' rolls. Benihana: 380 cal; Hissho 6pc: 345. Cream cheese adds ~70-100 cal vs a plain salmon roll.

Battered and fried shrimp, avocado, cucumber, rice. The tempura batter + frying adds ~100-150 cal over a plain shrimp roll. Kabuki 6pc: 348; Wegmans 8pc: 330; Kona Grill: 290. Often drizzled with eel sauce (add 30-40 more cal).
Deep-fried soft shell crab, avocado, cucumber, rice, often with eel sauce and spicy mayo. One of the heavier 'classic' rolls. Estimated from USDA soft shell crab tempura + standard roll components.

Shrimp tempura inside, avocado + eel (unagi) draped on top, eel sauce drizzle. A calorie bomb disguised as beautiful presentation. RA Sushi: 450; Benihana: 510; Southern Tsunami: 507. The eel sauce alone is 30-40 cal of pure sugar.

California roll base with assorted raw fish (tuna, salmon, yellowtail, shrimp) draped on top. The extra fish actually HELPS the macro profile — more protein per calorie than a plain California roll. Estimated from CA roll + 3-4oz assorted sashimi.

Typically a California roll base baked with spicy mayo, eel sauce, and crunchy topping. The baked mayo topping adds 100-150 cal of pure fat. Sarku Japan: 1841 cal for a FULL ORDER (multiple rolls). Per single roll ~490.

Entire roll is battered and deep fried, usually with cream cheese inside. The deep frying adds 150-200 cal from oil. This is essentially a cream cheese roll turned into a donut. Estimated from Philadelphia roll base + deep fry batter absorption.

Spicy tuna or shrimp tempura inside, topped with tempura crunch flakes and spicy mayo. The crunch flakes are fried batter bits — empty calories. Hissho Sushi crunchy roll package: 2176 cal (that's multiple rolls). Single roll estimate ~390.

Shrimp tempura inside, avocado outside, topped with thinly sliced raw fish and multiple sauces (eel sauce + spicy mayo). Similar to Dragon Roll but with more sauce. Specialty American-style roll — calorie bomb territory.

Typically salmon/tuna inside, topped with baked spicy crab and mayo mixture, then torched. The baked mayo topping is the calorie killer. Think of it as a Volcano Roll variant. Estimated from base roll + baked mayo crab topping.

Entire roll is tempura battered and fried, with multiple fillings (usually shrimp tempura + cream cheese or crab), drenched in eel sauce and spicy mayo. The worst macro profile of any roll — high carb from double batter + rice, high fat from frying + mayo.

Any 'deep fried' or 'tempura' specialty roll. Rule of thumb: take the non-fried version and add 150-200 cal from oil absorption. If it also has mayo/eel sauce drizzle, add another 60-100 cal. These are 500-800 cal range depending on size.

Raw salmon over pressed rice. Much better protein-to-cal ratio than rolls because less rice per piece. Benihana: 80 cal/2pc (smaller); Southern Tsunami: 136 cal/2pc (larger). Mid-range estimate. Each piece is ~1oz rice + 0.5oz fish.
Raw tuna over pressed rice. Leaner than salmon nigiri because tuna has less fat. Best protein-per-calorie nigiri option. USDA tuna: 31 cal/oz with 7g protein + ~60 cal rice per piece.
Cooked shrimp over pressed rice. Lowest fat nigiri option. Shrimp is one of the leanest proteins available (~7 cal per medium shrimp). Good option for calorie-conscious ordering.
Grilled freshwater eel with sweet eel sauce over rice. Higher cal than other nigiri because eel is fattier AND it comes pre-glazed with sugary eel sauce. The sauce adds ~30 cal of sugar per 2 pieces.
Raw yellowtail over pressed rice. Similar to salmon nigiri in fat content — yellowtail is a moderately fatty fish. Rich buttery flavor makes it feel indulgent but macros are reasonable.

THE lean option. Pure protein, zero carbs, zero rice. FatSecret: 41 cal/piece. 5 slices = 205 cal with 31g protein. Compare: a California Roll is 255 cal with only 9g protein. Sashimi wins on every macro metric.

The BEST protein-per-calorie option on any sushi menu. 155 cal for 33g protein. That's bodybuilder-grade macro efficiency. FatSecret: 31 cal/piece with 6.6g protein. Virtually zero fat. Order this if you're cutting.
Fattier than tuna sashimi but still excellent macros. Zero carbs, high protein. The fat is mostly omega-3s. Estimated from USDA yellowtail/amberjack raw data.

Typically 15 slices: 5 salmon + 5 tuna + 5 yellowtail. 540 cal for 90g protein — that's an entire day's protein for some people in one appetizer-priced dish. The smartest order at any sushi restaurant for fat loss.

Rice base (~1.5 cups = 300 cal), raw tuna (~4oz = 120 cal), avocado (100 cal), edamame (60 cal), cucumber, seaweed, sesame oil, soy-based sauce. FatSecret generic: 611 cal; Bonefish Grill: 630. The rice is more than HALF the calories.

Same as tuna poke but salmon has more fat (~4g more per serving). Freshii salmon poke: 850 cal (heavy on sauce/toppings). True Food Kitchen: 560. Our estimate is a typical build-your-own with standard toppings.

The spicy mayo mixed into the tuna adds ~100 cal of fat compared to a regular tuna poke. Plus most places drizzle MORE spicy mayo on top. If you see 'spicy' on a poke menu, assume +100-150 cal from mayo alone.

Tofu replaces fish — less protein, similar calories. The tofu is often marinated in sweet soy (extra sugar). Not the protein powerhouse people assume. You lose 15-20g protein vs the fish version for barely any calorie savings.

items.dish_name: Lean Poke Bowl (Greens Base, Light Sauce)
items.notes: THE SMART ORDER: Greens base (saves 250-300 cal vs rice), double protein, light ponzu instead of creamy sauce, extra cucumber/edamame. Same satisfaction, 40% fewer calories. Ask for sauce on the side. This is a genuine fat-loss meal.

items.dish_name: Loaded Poke Bowl (Rice, All Toppings, Creamy Sauce)
items.notes: Cheesecake Factory Ahi poke: 1040 cal. Freshii with all toppings: 860. When you pick rice + avocado + mango + crispy onions + spicy mayo + eel sauce + masago, you've built a 1000+ cal bowl. This is what most people actually order when they think they're 'eating healthy at a poke place.'

Almost free calories. Tofu + seaweed + dashi broth. Restaurant versions are lighter than homemade (Benihana: 30 cal; Kona Grill: 60 cal). High sodium (~600-900mg) but great for starting a meal — fills you up before the rolls arrive.

Excellent appetizer — high protein + fiber means you'll eat fewer rolls. Trader Joe's: 100 cal/half cup; Publix: 100 cal/cup in pods. The fiber/protein combo makes this one of the smartest sushi restaurant starters.

Looks healthy but the sesame oil dressing adds more calories than expected. Azuma Gourmet half cup: 110 cal. Still a reasonable side. Watch for the sugar in the sweet dressing — some versions have 8-10g sugar.
Per piece. Standard appetizer order is 5-6 pieces = 175-210 cal. Steamed is the way to go. Mostly wrapper carbs + small amount of pork filling. Not great protein but not terrible as a starter.
Per piece. Frying adds ~13 cal per piece vs steamed (37% more calories for same food). 6 fried gyoza = 288 cal vs 210 steamed. FatSecret pork gyoza: 250 cal/serving. Always choose steamed.

Per piece. Restaurant-sized tempura shrimp are larger than generic. Tiger Thai: 70 cal/piece. Costco: 70 cal/piece. A typical appetizer of 5 shrimp = 300 cal. The batter doubles the calories of plain shrimp.

THE HIDDEN KILLER. Mayo + sriracha = almost pure fat at 100 cal/tbsp. Most specialty rolls get 1-2 tbsp drizzled on top. A Dragon Roll with heavy spicy mayo can add 200 cal you'd never guess. Always ask for it on the side.

Basically soy sauce + mirin + sugar reduced to a glaze. Zero fat but HIGH sugar — 6g per tablespoon. It's liquid sugar being drizzled on your roll. Not as calorie-dense as spicy mayo but adds up on multi-roll orders. CalorieKing Southern Tsunami: ~30 cal/tbsp.

THE SMART SAUCE CHOICE. Citrus soy — tons of flavor for only 10 cal/tbsp. Compare: spicy mayo is 10x the calories. Use ponzu instead of spicy mayo and save 90 cal per tablespoon. Kikkoman ponzu: 10 cal/tbsp.
Basically zero-calorie flavor. The only concern is sodium (~900mg per tbsp). Low-sodium versions cut that in half. Use freely for flavor if you're not sodium-sensitive. Kikkoman: 10 cal/tbsp.

Mayo-based Japanese steakhouse sauce. Not as heavy as spicy mayo but still a calorie trap. Ying's: 48 cal/tbsp; Terry Ho's: 75 cal/tbsp; Kroger: 60 cal/tbsp. Mid-range estimate. Often served in generous portions at hibachi-style restaurants.

THE CALORIE FOUNDATION. Every standard roll contains ~200 cal of rice. This is why 2 rolls = 400 cal JUST in rice before any fish, avocado, or sauce. The rice vinegar + sugar seasoning adds ~30 cal over plain rice. Sashimi eliminates this entirely.

Much less rice than a roll — each nigiri piece has about 1oz of pressed rice (~35 cal). This is why 4 nigiri pieces have LESS rice calories than 1 roll. Nigiri is a smarter rice-to-protein ratio than rolls.

Hand rolls use LESS rice than cut rolls (~half). The cone shape means more fish-to-rice ratio. 2 hand rolls ≈ 1 regular roll in rice, but similar fish. A sneaky way to eat 'sushi rolls' with fewer carbs.

REALITY CHECK: A typical sushi dinner of 2 specialty rolls = 765 cal BEFORE any appetizer, miso soup, or edamame. Add a side and sake, and you're at 1000+ cal. The rice alone in 2 rolls = 400 cal (52% of total). Most people order 2-3 rolls.
